coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots and ruthenium doped oxygen sensitive nanofibrous membranes for monitoring the respiration of agricultural products
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Yulong
Yang, Di
Huo, Shaosen
Ren, Junkai
Gao, Ningxiao
Chen, Zhiyuan
Liu, Yong
Xie, Zheng
Zhou, Shuyun
Qu, Xiaozhong
coredata.dc:description: During the storage of agricultural products, the oxygen concentration in the packages represents the level of food respiration and hence indicates its freshness. Herein, self-referenced photoluminescent oxygen sensitive fibrous membranes were prepared, with the inclusion of an oxygen-sensitive probe, i.e. tris (4,7-diphenyl-1,10-phenanthroline) ruthenium (II) dichloride (RuDPP), with an oxygen-insensitive luminophore, i.e. silane-functionalized carbon dots (SiCDs). Silicone was selected as the matrix, obtained via sol-gel process, and was electrospun to get nanofibers containing mesoporous microstructure and with high oxygen permeability. The as-prepared RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 fibrous membranes possessed hydrophobicity and exhibited ideal oxygen sensitivity, which realized a remote evaluation of the freshness of packaged fruits, through ratiometric photoluminescence imaging on the concentration change of environmental oxygen. The results indicate that such kind of oxygen-sensitive fiber membranes are capable as an optical oxygen sensor for agricultural applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrospun fibrous membrane
Oxygen sensitive nanofibers
Optical sensor
Ratiometric imaging
Food respiration monitoring

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent photographs of RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 fibrous membranes with RuDPP content of (a) 0.28 wt%, (b) 0.16 wt%, (c) 0.023 wt%, (d) 0.008 wt% and (e) 0 wt% respectively. The excitation wavelength was 365 nm.

SEM images of RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibrous membranes containing (a) 0.28 wt%, (b) 0.16 wt%, (c) 0.023 wt%, (d) 0.008 wt% and (e) 0 wt% of RuDPP, and (f) the column chart of their average fiber diameter.
FTIR spectra of SiCDs, silicone (SiO2) nanofibers and RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibers.
Water contact angle of RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibrous membranes.
(a) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm and (b) the pore size distributions of RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibrous membranes.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of SiCDs, RuDPP and Ru@SiCDs-4 nanofibers and (b) RuDPP@ SiCDs/SiO2 with different RuDPP content.
Fluorescence photographs of (a) Ru@SiCDs-4, (b) Ru@SiCDs-3, (c) Ru@SiCDs-2 and (d) Ru@SiCDs-1 fibrous membranes at 0% and 21% oxygen concentration. The excitation wavelength was 360 nm.

Photoluminescence emission spectra of RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibers with (a–b) 0.28 wt%, (c–d) 0.16 wt%, and (e–f) 0.008 wt% of RuDPP at different oxygen concentration excited at 360 nm (a,c,e) and 470 nm (b,d,f) light.

Effect of oxygen concentration on the luminescence of fibrous membranes. (a) Photoluminescence intensity of SiCDs (λem = 430 nm, B) and RuDPP (λem = 600 nm, R) for the RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibers at different O2 concentration. (b) Calibration curves of Stern-Volmer plot of the fiber membranes. (c) Calibration plots of intensity ratio (B/R) of the fibrous membranes.

(a) Pixel intensity of the blue and red gray images for the RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 fibrous membranes at different O2 concentrations by DLS camera. (b) Calibration plots of intensity ratio (B/R) of the fiber membranes with mono-exponential fitting.

(a) Ratiometric imaging of RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibrous membranes at the slide under N2 flowing from a tube. (b) Photo of a fruit storage package equipped with the oxygen sensing fiber membrane. (c) Fluorescent photos of fibrous membranes at different days during fruit storage.
Illustration of the preparation of RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibrous membrane and the principle of the optical oxygen sensing.
Preparation parameters of RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 sols for electrospinning.

K
sv and standard error values of the RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibers.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots and ruthenium doped oxygen sensitive nanofibrous membranes for monitoring the respiration of agricultural products

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Composites, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Conversion and Optoelectronic Materials
Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Conversion and Optoelectronic Materials, Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Mechanical and Electric Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Mechanical and Electric Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Mechanical and Electric Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Y. Xu and D. Yang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: During the storage of agricultural products, the oxygen concentration in the packages represents the level of food respiration and hence indicates its freshness. Herein, self-referenced photoluminescent oxygen sensitive fibrous membranes were prepared, with the inclusion of an oxygen-sensitive probe, i.e. tris (4,7-diphenyl-1,10-phenanthroline) ruthenium (II) dichloride (RuDPP), with an oxygen-insensitive luminophore, i.e. silane-functionalized carbon dots (SiCDs). Silicone was selected as the matrix, obtained via sol-gel process, and was electrospun to get nanofibers containing mesoporous microstructure and with high oxygen permeability. The as-prepared RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 fibrous membranes possessed hydrophobicity and exhibited ideal oxygen sensitivity, which realized a remote evaluation of the freshness of packaged fruits, through ratiometric photoluminescence imaging on the concentration change of environmental oxygen. The results indicate that such kind of oxygen-sensitive fiber membranes are capable as an optical oxygen sensor for agricultural applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Fresh agricultural products are perishable, seasonal and regional, which brings great challenges in storage, market supply and product development. The preservation of agricultural products is a very important link in the production safety of agricultural products. During storage, the variation of oxygen level in package is an important indicator of the stuff freshness as O2 is the reactant of food respiration [1,2]. Currently, the common method for oxygen monitoring is electrochemical sensing. Although the electrochemical sensors may have high sensitivity and selectivity [3,4], the application is limited in scope due to the disadvantages of invasive, consumption of oxygen and expensive. Comparably, optical oxygen sensors have attracted increasing attention so far in the fields of food packaging [5–7], wound imaging [8–10], smart textile [11,12] and micro-environment monitoring [13] etc., due to the advantages of facile use, low cost, real-time and non-invasive.

Nowadays, a promising approach for spatiotemporal oxygen mapping combines dual luminophore referencing sensing and ratiometric imaging [14,15]. Besides, for monitoring the oxygen level in air, it is efficient to introduce oxygen-sensitive luminophores into a solid matrix, which could be made from polymers [15], ceramics [16] and nature macromolecules [17], and could appear various topologies like nanoparticle [9], fiber [18] and film [10]. Among them, polymer-based matrices have been widely studied [19,20]. However, their application could be seriously influenced by the oxygen permeability and chemical stability of the type of materials [21]. Moreover, the optical performance may also declines if the fluorescent probes were physically dispersed into the polymer matrix, for example, the occurrence of self-quenching, photochemical reaction or leakage [22]. Compared to common polymers, organically modified silicate materials or silicones have a brighter prospect for optical oxygen sensor, mainly because of its oxygen permeability, and also the indigenous properties of mesoporous containing, optical transmittance, better chemical stability and good mechanical property [16,23–26]. Besides, the processing of silicate materials is also flexible. In particular, electrospinning is applicable to make silicone nanofibers with high specific surface area and porosity, ideally for using as a solid-substrate of gas sensors [19,20,22].

Herein, we report on the utilization of the electrospinning technique to fabricate silicone nanofibers and furthermore the fibrous membranes, doped by an oxygen sensitive molecule, i.e. tris (4,7-diphenyl-1,10-phenanthroline) ruthenium (II) dichloride (RuDPP) together with silane-functionalized carbon dots (SiCDs), in order to harvest a highly responsive membrane for the detection of environmental oxygen concentration by measuring the emission intensity ratio of the ruthenium complex to CDs (Scheme 1 ). The oxygen sensitivity of ruthenium complex relies on dynamic quenching of the excited state of the luminophore by oxygen, which leads to the change of luminescence intensity or lifetime with oxygen content [27]. And luminescent CDs are also attracting increasing attentions in recent years. As a probe, CDs have high quantum yield, excellent photostability as well as good biocompatibility, and are easily functionalized [28,29]. For instance, Xie et al. [30] reported a kind of SiCDs and furthermore the preparation of gel glass by copolymerizing SiCDs with silane precursors, e.g. methyltriethoxysilane, which provided a way of chemically embedding the luminophores inside the resultant silicone products. This could gain uniform distribution of SiCDs in the matrix at molecular level and avoid the agglomeration or phase separation of SiCDs. Since the photoluminescence of SiCDs is not sensitive to oxygen, the intensity ratio of CDs to the oxygen-sensitive probe can correlate to oxygen concentration. In comparison with the measurement from emission intensity of individual oxygen probe, the ratiometric method certainly has advantages in remoting detection of local oxygen concentration change, because it can diminish the disruption from the objective movement or the change of camera angle, which would affect the recording of absolute luminescent intensity but not the intensity ratio. In this paper, we will demonstrate that the photoluminescent silicone membranes, formed from the electrospun nanofibers, exhibited excellent oxygen sensitivity and hence preferable capacity on observing the freshness of storage fruit.

Tris (4,7-diphenyl-1,10-phenanthroline) ruthenium (II) dichloride (RuDPP, CAS: 36309-88-3) was purchased by Alfa Aesar company. Anhydrous citric acid, N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl methyldimethoxy silane (AEAPMS), petroleum ether and acetic ether were purchased from Beijing Chemical Factory (Beijing, China). Anhydrous ethanol was purchased from Beijing Tong Guang Fine Chemical Company (Beijing, China). Methyltriethoxysilane (MTES) was purchased from Nanjing Kun Cheng Chemical Co., Ltd (Jiangsu, China). Distilled water was manufactured by the lab. All materials are used as received without further purification.

SiCDs were synthesized via a one-pot method by the pyrolysis of citric acid and using multiaminosilane AEAPMS as a coordinating solvent [30] (Scheme S1, Supplemental Material). Briefly, 0.5 g of anhydrous citric acid, 10 mL of AEAPMS and 10 mL of distilled water were mixed thoroughly and placed into Teflon lined stainless autoclave. The autoclave was heated to 180 °C in an electric oven and kept for 12 h. The final products were purified by precipitating with the mixture of petroleum ether and acetic ether (5/1, v/v) three times or using chromatography column packed with silica gel.

The SiCDs/SiO2 sols were first prepared by the hydrolysis and condensation polymerization of MTES with SiCDs in acidic catalyst condition (Scheme 1 and S1). MTES was dissolved in ethanol and distilled water in a molar ratio of 1: 6: 5. Here, 0.75 g of SiCDs, 40.375 mL of MTES and 80 mL of anhydrous ethanol were mixed into a 250 mL beaker under vigorous stirring. Then a total of 20 mL of aqueous acetic acid solution (pH = 2.5) was divided into three parts, and was added drop by drop into the beaker with vigorous stirring at every half an hour respectively. SiCDs/SiO2 sol was obtained after continuous stirring for 12 h at room temperature and then evaporated to two-third (2/3) initial volume (in vacuum, 35 °C). To regain its initial volume, a certain amount of ethanol was added to the sol and stirred again. Then, different weights of RuDPP were respectively added into the SiCDs/SiO2 sols to obtain RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 composite sols. The RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 sols were stirred openly at room temperature for further sol-gel reaction until the viscosity increased to an appropriate value, before the as-synthesized sols were put into an electrospinning apparatus to prepare nanofibers. The specific preparation parameters were shown in Table 1 . According to the content of RuDPP content, these sols were named as Ru@SiCDs-0, Ru@SiCDs-1, Ru@SiCDs-2, Ru@SiCDs-3, and Ru@SiCDs-4 respectively.

For electrospinning, the RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 sol was placed into a 10 mL syringe (Jiangsu Zhiyu Medical Instrument Co., LTD, Taixing, China) with a 21 gauge stainless needle. The syringe was put on a constant current pump (LongerPump, Baoding, China) with a flow of 0.8 mL/h. A positive voltage of +20 kV was applied to the aluminized collector while a negative voltage of −2.5 kV was applied to the needle. The RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 fibers were collected on an aluminized paper which was placed at a distance of 15 cm from the needle. The as-prepared fiber membranes were dried in vacuum for 24 h to remove residual solvent.

Digital photographs were obtained by a digital camera (D5100, Nikon, Japan) under daylight and UV light (360 nm), respectively. The morphology of RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 fibers was observed by scanning electron microscope (SEM, HITACHI S4700, Japan) at 20 kV after coating with platinum. Fiber diameters were measured by ImageJ software and processed by Origin. The RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 fibers were ground with KBr powder and the transmittance of each resulting pellet was measured using a Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrophotometer (Nicoiet8700, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The hydrophobicity of membranes was tested by a water contact angle meter (OCA20, Dataphysics, Germany). The nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms were measured at 150 °C using an analyzer (ASAP 2020, Micromeritics Co., Ltd, USA). Surface areas were calculated by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. UV–vis spectra of RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofiber membranes were recorded on a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (LS55, Agilent Technologies, USA). The viscosity of the electrospun sol was measured by a laminar viscometer (Brookfield viscometer, DV3T, USA).

A tailored rectangular RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 fiber membrane was placed in the quartz sample cell and fixed on the inner wall of the cell. After that, different flows of pure nitrogen and pure oxygen gas were continuously introduced into the cell, and the oxygen concentration in the cell was adjusted by controlling the flow rates of the two kinds of gas. The blow-off time was maintained at more than 5 min each time. After the completion of the aeration, the quartz cell was sealed and placed on a fluorescence spectrometer (LS55, Agilent Technologies, USA) for excitation at 360 nm and 470 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxygen ratiometric imaging and fruit storage test
ce:section.ce:para: A schematic view of a manual and simple fruit storage device for O2 sensing was shown in Scheme S2. The fiber membrane was cut into a rectangular shape and adhered to the inner wall of the conical flask along the edge of the fiber membrane using the scotch tape. Then fresh grapes were stored hermetically in the conical flask and stored in dark. After predetermined time intervals, the fiber membrane was irradiated at a fixed position using an ultraviolet lamp, and its fluorescent photographs were taken by a digital camera (D5100, Nikon, Japan).

As shown in Fig. 1 , RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibrous membranes, containing 0.28 wt%, 0.16 wt%, 0.023 wt%, 0.008 wt% and 0 wt% of RuDPP respectively, emit different color light from SiCDs and RuDPP after being excited by ultraviolet light at 365 nm. As the content of RuDPP increasing, the luminescence color of the RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibers with the same SiCDs content gradually changed from bright blue to purple. The change of color was caused by the difference in the fluorescence intensity of the red light emitted by the RuDPP in composite fibers. Confocal laser scanning microscopy revealed relatively homogeneous luminescence from individual fibers, implying a uniform distribution of SiCDs and RuDPP in the silicone matrix, without obvious aggregation (Figure S1 and S2).

From the SEM images in Fig. 2 , it can be seen that RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibrous membranes had good morphology, and the change of the content of the RuDPP had no obvious effect on the morphology of the fibers. The fiber diameters were counted by ImageJ software and the average diameters of the nanofiber membranes were determined to be 1.01 ± 0.25 μm, 1.17 ± 0.49 μm, 0.50 ± 0.20 μm, 0.64 ± 0.24 μm and 0.76 ± 0.23 μm, respectively (Fig. 2f and Table S1). Actually, our previous work [31] had confirmed that the viscosity of the sol was the major key for determining the morphology of fibers in the case of the same electrospinning condition. Therefore, appropriate viscosity of the sol facilitated to prepare uniform and fine fibers, which might increase the oxygen permeability of the fibrous membranes and shorten the response time.

The FT-IR spectra of SiCDs, SiO2 nanofiber, and RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 composite nanofiber are shown in Fig. 3 , where the spectrum of SiCDs shows obvious absorption bands at 1655 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1, corresponding to the C=O stretch vibration and N-H bending vibration respectively from the amide group on the surface of SiCDs. These characteristic peaks from SiCDs were also observed in the FTIR spectrum of RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibers, which indicated the successful incorporation grafting by the chemical bond of Si-O-Si of SiCDs into the silicone (SiO2) nanofibers.

The hydrophobicity of the membranes were demonstrated in Fig. 4 , showing that water contact angles are over 130°. The hydrophobicity is resulted from low surface energy from the hydrophobic Si-CH3 group and the rough electrospun fibrous structure [32]. The hydrophobic behavior allows the nanofiber membranes to effectively block the contamination of sewage, thereby the influence of the user environment on its fluorescence performance could be minimized.

The porous structure of the RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibers in the membranes were tested using nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm (Fig. 5 a). The results show that the specific surface area of the nanofiber membrane is 15.22 m2/g, the average pore diameter is 5.24 nm, and the pore volume is 1.99 × 10−2 cm3/g. Fig. 5b further demonstrates the pore size distribution of the RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 composite nanofibers, where two peaks at 1.9 nm and 3.8 nm are revealed, which indicate that the fibers have both microporous and mesoporous structures. Compared to the coating film, the electrospun RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 fiber membrane with the hierarchical pore structure has larger specific surface area and porosity, and thus it should be more favorable for oxygen diffusion and penetration.

The UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 6 show that SiCDs have two absorption peaks centered at 260 nm and 360 nm, and RuDPP also displays two absorption features centered at 290 nm and 460 nm. The absorption peaks of RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibers are almost consistent with the SiCDs and RuDPP, which further indicates that the SiCDs and RuDPP are incorporated successfully into the SiO2 nanofibers. The luminescence excitation and emission spectra of RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibers are given in Figure S3a and S3b. In the excitation range of 340–500 nm, it confirms that the fibers exhibit almost identical emission peaks at 430 nm from the SiCDs and 590 nm from the RuDPP (Figure S4), besides the emission wavelength of SiCDs fully overlaps the excitation of RuDPP (Figure S3), indicating possible fluorescence resonance energy transfer effect between the two luminophores. This may explain that the RuDPP peak at 590 nm displays the strongest intensity with λex = 320 nm in addition to λex = 440 nm in the luminescence spectra of RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibers, while the 430 nm peak (SiCDs) gains the strongest intensity at λex = 360 nm (Figure S5). In addition to the above, it should be also noticed that being excited by UV light, the gap between the two mission peaks is more than 150 nm, which will favor the ratiometric imaging from wavelength-separated channels.

Fluorescent photographs of the RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofiber membranes at oxygen concentrations of 21% and 0% are shown in Fig. 7 . The RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibers under different oxygen concentrations showed a significant change of luminescence color. The nanofibers with 0.28 wt% and 0.16 wt% RuDPP exhibit pink due to the mixture of obvious red and weak blue. As the oxygen concentration decreases, the fluorescence intensity of the RuDPP increases and the emitted red light became more pronounced, whereby the fluorescent color of the fiber membrane changed to deep red.

As shown in Fig. 8 , the luminescent emission intensity of RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofiber membranes increased as the level of oxygen decreased. The ratio of luminescence intensities in oxygen-free to oxygen-saturated (I0/I100) RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibers was about 3.0. Compared to the electrospun fibrous membranes made from common polymers (Table S2), the I0/I100 of RuDPP in the as-prepared silicone-based fibrous membranes are much higher, e.g. 2.17 for PS vs. ~3.0 for RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2, attributed to the difference on the oxygen permeability of the polymer matrices.

It is shown in Fig. 9 a that the intensity peaks of the SiCDs (at 430 nm) from RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibers with 0.28 wt%, 0.16 wt% and 0.008 wt% of RuDPP are basically stable at different oxygen concentration, while the intensity peaks of RuDPP (at 600 nm) gradually decreased with increasing in O2 concentration. For ratiometric imaging, the non-oxygen sensing property of SiCDs as a reference luminophore shows good optical stability. Moreover, the RuDPP in the fibers exhibited higher oxygen sensitivity at the range of oxygen concentration below 21%, which reveals that the fibers are especially sensitive to oxygen under lower oxygen levels. The Stern-Volmer plots of RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofibers for oxygen concentrations in the range of 0–100% were evaluated (Fig. 9b). As shown in Table 2 , the K
sv values for the fibers with 0.28 wt%, 0.16 wt% and 0.008 wt% of RuDPP are 0.949, 0.965, and 0.998, respectively. The calibration plots of intensity ratio (B/R) for oxygen concentrations in the range of 0%-100% were also evaluated for further testing feasibility of the ratiometric measurement (Fig. 9c). The R
2 values of ratiometric method for the fibers with 0.28 wt%, 0.16 wt% and 0.008 wt% of RuDPP are 0.959, 0.985, and 0.998, respectively (Table 2). The plot of the fiber membrane with 0.008 wt% of RuDPP reveals better linearity. Overall, the result indicates that the ratiometric method using the luminescence intensity ratio of SiCDs to RuDPP for oxygen determination is feasible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxygen ratiometric imaging and fruit storage experiment
ce:section.ce:para:
The monitoring of oxygen concentration and distribution by ratiometric imaging using a digital camera instead of the costly optical spectrometer is really practical for food storage. In order to visualize the spatial distribution of oxygen, the fluorescence images (RGB) of the RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofiber membranes under different oxygen pressure (
P
O 2
) were taken by the camera in a simple self-assembly device (Scheme S2). The blue (B) and red (R) channels in the RGB photographs were respectively extracted by ImageJ software and the emission intensity of B and R channel was read out by ImageJ pro plus (Figure S6). The R, G or B gray photos extracted from an RGB image respectively reflect the intensity value of the Red, Green or Blue color at every pixel point in this image. So, the R channel gray value that represented red fluorescence intensity of the RuDPP emitting at around 600 nm decreases with increasing
P
O 2
(Fig. 10 a). On the other hand, The B channel gray value that represented blue fluorescence intensity of the SiCDs emitting at around 430 nm barely changed with the oxygen concentration (Fig. 10a). As shown in Fig. 10b, the ratio of the average pixel gray value of the B channel to that of the R channel was used for O2 measurement by the ratiometric method. After mono-exponential fitting, the fitting equation (Equation (1)) of the RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 fiber membranes with 0.008 wt% RuDPP was obtained with a R2 of 0.999.
(1)
p Air
( mmHg )
=
627.78 × ln
[
( B/R - 1.178 )
/ 2.293 ]

According to the calibration equation (Equation (1)), the value of B/R at every pixel in the RGB image taken by the camera is converted into a corresponding oxygen concentration value (or oxygen partial pressure value). After software programming, the B/R gray images are converted into pseudo-color photographs with an oxygen concentration distribution strip according to the calculation of the calibration equation (Figure S6). According to the oxygen concentration distribution strip, oxygen concentration and distribution on the surface of the fiber membrane can be visually observed (Fig. 11 a).

As shown in Fig. 11c, the fluorescent color of the fiber membrane varied with different storage times. The fiber membrane emitted brilliant blue fluorescence at the beginning. When stored on the third day, the fluorescent color changed to purple which caused by the respiration of grapes. With the process of respiration, the fluorescent color became light red on the 6th and 7th day of storage. And there was no significant difference in color, indicating that the oxygen in the conical flask had been exhausted.

In summary, RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 fibrous membranes were successfully prepared by the combination of electrospinning and sol-gel condensation polymerization techniques. The average diameter of the fibers is between 0.76 and 1.17 μm. With surface silane modification, SiCDs were well-dispersed in the oxygen permeable silicone matrix through the formation of chemical bonding, working as a reference probe to allow the ratiometric imaging with the oxygen sensitive dye, i.e. RuDPP. Besides, the RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 nanofiber membranes exhibited high porosity and hydrophobicity that also benefited their responsibility to the oxygen concentration. The visual reading of
P
O 2
value and its distribution were realized by transforming the digital fluorescence photo to the pseudo-color image. These features were utilized favorably for remote monitoring the respiration of packaged agricultural products. Our results imply that the electrospun RuDPP@SiCDs/SiO2 fiber membranes are promising as optical sensor of the determination of local oxygen concentration.

Yulong Xu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Di Yang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Shaosen Huo: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Junkai Ren: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Ningxiao Gao: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Zhiyuan Chen: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Yong Liu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Zheng Xie: Conceptualization, Supervision, Validation, Writing - review & editing. Shuyun Zhou: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Xiaozhong Qu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Supervision, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper..

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Number 21374008, 51873214).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.polymertesting.2020.106957.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Influence of low O2 and high CO2 environment on changes in metabolite concentrations in harvested vegetable soybeans

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Makino, A. Nishizaka, M. Yoshimura, I. Sotome, K. Kawai, T. Akihiro, Influence of low O2 and high CO2 environment on changes in metabolite concentrations in harvested vegetable soybeans, Food Chem.. 317 (2020) 126380.
